# RFC-0004: Measurement And Toolchain Contract

> **Version:** 0.4.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0004:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0004c-summary"></a>

This RFC governs measurements and the toolchains that execute them: Eval and Bench semantics and result normalization, the Inferlab-owned release-versioned measurement toolchains, and workload-attached Nsight Systems profiling. Serve profiles, recipe cases, server records, and readiness semantics are defined by [RFC-0003](../rfc/RFC-0003.md); records by [RFC-0005](../rfc/RFC-0005.md). [RFC-0001](../rfc/RFC-0001.md) holds the specification overview and the authority map.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0004:C-MEASUREMENTS] Eval And Bench Measurements (Normative) <a id="rfc-0004c-measurements"></a>

Inferlab MUST support reusable workspace-local Eval and serving-Bench definitions.

An Eval definition MUST be able to express the evaluated task or dataset selection, bounded sample policy, primary metric, pass threshold, and timeout when those values apply.

A serving-Bench definition MUST be able to express prompt-source parameters, input and output shape, concurrency-limited load, request-rate-limited load, arrival burstiness, request-count or duration policy, prefix-cache reset policy, and timeout when those values apply.

A recipe MUST be able to select an Eval gate and one or more Eval and Bench measurements.

A Bench guarded by an Eval gate MUST be skipped when that gate does not succeed.

An OpenAI-compatible completions smoke definition MUST express its prompt, maximum-token limit, and timeout independently of a server endpoint or execution engine.

The Eval definition kind MUST determine the execution boundary without a separate user-selectable engine setting.

An OpenAI-compatible completions smoke measurement MUST NOT require or launch an installed measurement-toolchain runtime.

The smoke request MUST use the resolved public endpoint and served model, the declared prompt and maximum-token limit, non-streaming generation, and zero temperature.

A successful smoke measurement MUST receive a 2xx HTTP response containing a JSON object with a non-empty `choices` array whose first element contains a string `text` value.

A transport error, timeout, interruption, non-2xx response, malformed JSON response, missing or empty `choices` array, or a missing or non-string first-choice `text` value MUST fail the smoke measurement.

Every smoke attempt MUST preserve the effective HTTP request, elapsed time and terminal outcome, plus failure evidence when the outcome is failure.

When the HTTP operation returns a complete response, smoke evidence MUST additionally preserve its status, body size, and raw body artifact.

When a valid `choices` array is parsed, smoke evidence MUST preserve its choice count.

lm-eval measurements MUST execute through the Eval runtime fixed by the invoking Inferlab release.

Serving Bench measurements MUST execute through the AIPerf runtime fixed by the invoking Inferlab release.

A measurement client result MUST carry result envelope version `1`.

The measurement runtime MUST reject an Eval or Bench result whose envelope version it does not support, failing that case with an error naming the unsupported version.

Static concurrency and request-rate definitions MUST expand into deterministic ordered cases before execution.

The adaptive policy `highest-passing-bisection-v1` MUST probe declared initial rates in ascending unique order.

A finite target metric value less than or equal to the threshold MUST classify a probe as passing.

After initial probes, the policy MUST bisect the bracket between the highest passing rate and the lowest higher failing rate.

The policy MUST stop at the refinement limit or when the configured minimum rate resolution is reached.

The policy MUST select the highest observed passing rate as its final rate.

The policy MUST select no final rate when no probe passes, a required probe fails, or the target metric is missing or non-finite.

Manual and closed-loop execution under the same invoking Inferlab release and toolchain identity MUST use the same case planning, reset, timeout, AIPerf version, result normalization, and evidence semantics for the same effective Bench definition and serving snapshot.

Each successful Bench case MUST report `completed_requests` and `failed_requests` as integer counts, with at least one completed request and zero failed requests.

Bench normalization schema `aiperf-summary-v1` MUST map AIPerf summary values as follows: `request_throughput.avg` to `request_throughput`, `output_token_throughput.avg` to `output_throughput`, `total_token_throughput.avg` to `total_token_throughput`, `request_latency.avg` to `mean_request_latency_ms`, `request_latency.p99` to `p99_request_latency_ms`, `time_to_first_token.avg` to `mean_ttft_ms`, `time_to_first_token.p99` to `p99_ttft_ms`, `inter_token_latency.avg` to `mean_itl_ms`, and `inter_token_latency.p99` to `p99_itl_ms`.

The normalization schema MUST preserve AIPerf's request window, token accounting, and percentile semantics for those source fields.

A successful Bench case MUST fail validation when a required normalized value is absent, non-numeric, or non-finite.

A failed Bench case MUST finalize as one failed Bench-record case with explicit error text, stdout and stderr paths, available request counts, partial raw artifacts, and an empty or explicitly partial metric set.

A failed Bench case MUST preserve process outcome when a measurement process started.

Prefix-cache reset policy MUST remain part of the Bench definition.

When reset is requested, Inferlab MUST execute the server-specific reset capability before each case.

When reset fails, that case MUST NOT start.

A reset failure record MUST preserve the reset request and failure evidence.

Inferlab MUST record each Eval or Bench measurement's identity, effective parameters, effective invocation, structured metrics, raw result artifacts, failure, and skip reason when applicable.

**Rationale:** Eval and serving performance are stable Inferlab measurement concepts even when serving frameworks change. The built-in smoke is an endpoint protocol check that needs no external evaluation engine, while lm-eval and AIPerf remain release-owned external engines for dataset evaluation and serving Bench. Keeping case, gate, reset, adaptive, lifecycle, and evidence semantics in Inferlab preserves one experiment workflow across those execution boundaries.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0004:C-INFERLAB-TOOLCHAIN] Inferlab Measurement Toolchain (Normative) <a id="rfc-0004c-inferlab-toolchain"></a>

The public CLI MUST provide `inferlab toolchain install` to install the lm-eval and Bench runtimes fixed by the running Inferlab release.

One explicit installation MUST provide independently isolated lm-eval and Bench dependency closures under one version-and-platform installation identity.

The installed Eval runtime MUST contain the release-pinned lm-eval engine.

The installed Bench runtime MUST contain the release-pinned AIPerf engine.

`toolchain install` and lm-eval or Bench preflight MUST support glibc Linux x86_64 and aarch64.

`toolchain install` and lm-eval or Bench preflight MUST fail with an explicit unsupported-platform error on other operating systems, C libraries, and architectures.

A workflow that selects neither lm-eval nor Bench MUST NOT require a supported measurement-toolchain platform or an installed measurement toolchain.

The toolchain MUST be installed directly at `$XDG_DATA_HOME/inferlab/toolchains/<inferlab-version>/<platform>/`, using the platform's conventional XDG data default when `XDG_DATA_HOME` is unset.

Installed prefixes MUST NOT be relocated after installation.

A complete matching installation MUST be identified by completion metadata that matches the invoking Inferlab version, host platform, release manifest and lock digests, runner digests and versions, and installed lm-eval and AIPerf versions.

Installation MUST serialize concurrent writers.

Installation MUST consume the package definition fixed by the Inferlab release without updating it.

Installation MUST verify both runtime closures before publishing completion metadata.

A matching complete installation MUST make `toolchain install` an idempotent no-op.

An explicit `toolchain install` MUST either replace an incomplete or release-mismatched installation with a complete matching installation or fail without publishing matching completion metadata.

Dry-run and experiment execution MUST only validate the matching installed runtime required by selected lm-eval or Bench measurements.

Dry-run and experiment execution MUST NOT install, repair, update, or select a different toolchain version.

When a selected lm-eval or Bench measurement requires a missing or incomplete matching runtime, dry-run and execution MUST fail before the first experiment effect and identify `inferlab toolchain install` as the required action.

Different Inferlab versions MUST be able to coexist. Automatic update, garbage collection, registry publication, and general-purpose toolchain management commands are outside this contract.

**Rationale:** Dataset evaluation and serving performance measurement use release-owned external engines rather than the model-serving dependency closure. One explicit versioned installation keeps workspaces small and serving locks stable, while isolated lm-eval and Bench closures prevent their unrelated dependencies and platform requirements from perturbing each other. The built-in OpenAI smoke remains available without this installation because it adds no external evaluation engine.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0004:C-WORKLOAD-PROFILING] Workload-Attached Nsight Systems Profiling (Normative) <a id="rfc-0004c-workload-profiling"></a>

When profiling is enabled for a server, Inferlab MUST prepare every launched process rank of each accelerator replica declared as profile-capable by the selected integration as an Nsight Systems capture target for later Eval or Bench measurements.

A serve profile, recipe case, or invocation-scoped server setting MAY request capture-target preparation.

A server prepared with capture targets MUST NOT bound its readiness wait by the serve profile's readiness timeout ([RFC-0003:C-RESOLUTION](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-resolution)): capture instrumentation multiplies startup cost by an unpredictable factor, and a budget chosen for uninstrumented startup converts progressing servers into false failures. The capture-armed readiness wait terminates only by readiness success, process-group exit, or operator interruption, each with deterministic evidence.

An Eval or Bench execution MUST support an explicit profiling-capture request without changing the selected named measurement definition.

A usable capture target MUST identify the target, its logical role, replica, process, and rank boundary, its supported framework-neutral window-control kinds, its control mechanism, its profiler storage, and its report finalization or retrieval strategy.

The selected framework integration MUST declare framework-specific profiler controls and launch inputs needed by a capture target.

Inferlab MUST retain authority for profiler process lifecycle, capture planning, window control, collection finalization, report verification, cleanup, and authoritative evidence.

A serve profile MAY declare profiler escape inputs, and each of its roles MAY declare escape inputs that merge into the profile's: scalar escapes replace, launch and start option lists concatenate with the role's options after the profile's, the trace set replaces, and environment entries merge per key with the role value winning.

Profiler escape option lists are declared separately for the profiler launch command and the collection-start command, and Inferlab MUST splice each list ahead of its managed argv tail so that a managed value wins on collision.

The profiler executable, trace set, sampling, and context-switch escapes are dedicated fields: the trace set replaces the managed default trace set, sampling and context switch each replace their managed default, and the free-list option form of a dedicated field MUST be rejected toward the dedicated field.

A profiler escape environment map MUST apply to both the profiler launch and collection-start commands: entries reach the wrapped server process through launch-command inheritance and remain profiler-only on the collection-start command. Environment keys MUST be POSIX identifiers, rejected at workspace load naming the escape field and the key; the rendered environment prefix terminates option parsing with a separator before the first assignment, so no key can be parsed as an option of the environment utility.

An escape option that names a managed profiler fact — session identity; report storage, export, and overwrite lifecycle; capture-range mechanics; or launch wait behavior — MUST be rejected when the workspace is loaded, naming the escape field and the offending option, in long, short, attached short-option-value, and abbreviated long forms — any strict prefix of a managed long option name, which either resolves to the managed option or is an ambiguity. A standalone `-` or `--` token in an escape option list MUST likewise be rejected at workspace load, because it ends option parsing and displaces the managed argv tail into positionals of the wrapped command.

The record MUST preserve both the raw escape declaration and the effective profiler invocations it produced.

Profiling an Eval or Bench MUST remain an observation mode attached to that measurement and MUST NOT create a separate workload kind.

A profiling request MUST bind to a managed server record and resolve every prepared process-rank target before measured client work starts.

Each capture window MUST have a semantic identity and a binding to the measured Eval phase or Bench case before the window opens.

A static capture plan for a range-backed target MUST enumerate every expected capture window and compute the expected range count for each selected target before collection is armed.

A static capture plan MUST preserve the mapping from each framework range index to its semantic capture-window identity.

A profiling request that uses a range-backed target MUST fail before measured client work when its capture windows cannot be enumerated in advance.

A dynamic measurement MAY use an external-session control kind instead when every selected target advertises that control kind.

Inferlab MUST open a capture window before its bound measured phase begins.

Inferlab MUST close the capture window after the measured phase completes or fails.

Inferlab MUST attempt to close an opened capture window when measured client work fails.

Framework window-control actions MUST honor the serve-profile fact `capture_control_deadline_seconds` as their response deadline, defaulting to 60 seconds when undeclared.

For asynchronous report generation, Inferlab MUST finalize managed profiler collection before verifying report coverage or performing destructive server cleanup.

After collection finalization, Inferlab MUST verify report coverage for every planned range on every required target.

A required report reference missing after collection finalization MUST be treated as capture failure.

A measurement requiring profiling MUST fail when target resolution, window-opening control, measured client work, collection finalization, required report generation, or required report verification fails.

A window-closing control failure MUST be preserved as evidence and adjudicated by report-coverage verification: the capture succeeds despite it when every required report verifies, and fails carrying both the coverage and control evidence when one does not.

Measurement evidence MUST preserve the capture plan, window identities, selected role, replica, process, and rank targets, range mapping, control actions, collection finalization, report references, verification outcomes, and failure evidence when those facts apply.

**Rationale:** Profiling observes ordinary correctness and performance work. Binding semantic windows to stable role, replica, process, and rank targets keeps traces comparable across single-role and multi-role topologies, while static range planning preserves the Nsight Systems repeat-count behavior needed by framework-native CUDA profiler controls. The capture-armed readiness wait is unbounded because the readiness loop already fails instantly on process-group death and cleanly on interruption, so a timeout's only unique detection is the alive-but-stuck class — while the failure such a budget actually produces is a progressing instrumented warmup killed by a limit written for uninstrumented startup. Window-opening control stays fail-fast because a lost start silently shifts every later range index off its planned window identity; window-closing control defers to report coverage because the report either landed or it did not, and a late stop response must not fail a capture whose evidence exists. Escape option lists stay split between launch and start because the qualified Nsight Systems option surfaces are disjoint where it matters — trace-shaping options exist only at launch while metrics, sampling, and capture-range options exist only at start — so a single list could not be spliced correctly. Managed facts stay managed because they are the static capture plan's spine: session identity binds control actions to their target, the report lifecycle underwrites coverage verification, and capture-range mechanics carry the planned range mapping. Dedicated fields for the trace set, sampling, and context switch dissolve the default-collision problem: those managed defaults exist to be replaced, so replacement flows through a typed field instead of an option-list collision rule. The environment map is asymmetric by mechanism, not policy: launch-command inheritance is the only escape channel that reaches the wrapped server, while the collection-start command touches only the profiler.

*Since: v0.1.0*
